sentences.text: Yes.
My name is Jacob, J-A-C-O-B, Nagiel, N-A-G-I-E-L.
Yankel, Yankel.
Date of birth is 14/12/1912.
Born in Warsaw.
In Poland.
Well, my father was Simon, and my mother was Helena.
And I have two brothers and three sisters.
The names, say, my brother is Andrew.
He's in the United States.
The second brother is Moshe.
He's in Tel Aviv, in Israel.
And the three sisters, at the time of the war-- they-- they--
They didn't survive.
Do you want the name of my sisters?
It is Fela, the oldest; Pola, the younger; and Ala, the youngest.
My father was a merchant in-- in [INAUDIBLE].
It depends on the-- on the-- OK, on the time.
Sometimes yes, sometimes worse or sometimes better, like any business.
Yes.
Oh, I remember where we lived close to-- to the-- to the River Vistula.
And I remember some-- some-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] some cartridges, cartridges fell through the window in-- in the-- in the-- in the wardrobes.
And for a long time, I remember one country, it was in a [INAUDIBLE] in state, so it was long time, yes.
Oh, yes, it was hard times, yes, for everybody, yes.
Traditional, but not very religious.
But we-- we used to-- in the-- in the house we used to eat kosher, of course.
And my mother was every Friday bench the-- the candles.
And my father went to synagogue, yes.
He used to take us too, of course, we were smaller.
When we were older, we-- we didn't attend much synagogue.
Oh, yes.
I have grandparents from Father's side, and-- and from the mother's side, and uncles, yes.
A lot of uncles, yes and--
Yes, mostly Warsaw.
But some lived in Otwock, not far from Warsaw.
And some immigrated to-- to Belgium, where they meet in Brussels.
Yes, yes.
It was-- the last letters were [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE] Street.
Mostly Polish.
Yes.
Always Polish, of course, Polish, yes.
Yes.
My father speak Yiddish.
My mother speak Yiddish.
But it was not a perfect Yiddish.
Later on, when I work for an organization but before the war, I learned Yiddish, right Yiddish.
More-- more literate Yiddish.
No, no, no, not writing, but only reading--
Only-- only talking.
More correct, Yiddish, yes.
Oh, of course.
It's the-- it's the way of life.
It was normal.
Oh, no special incidents, but always, Jewish, [NON-ENGLISH], and so forth.
Not especially, sometimes yes, but not-- not too much.
Because we-- we were mostly lived within Jewish children, and-- and families.
So we don't meet much Polish people, only in the schools, of course.
No, their Polish school, yes.
The primary, Polish school, yes.
But it was many Jewish children there.
Not much, very little.
Yes, I-- I-- I attended cheder for a few months or something, yes.
Oh, yes.
Yes, I have a bar mitzvah, yes.
Yes, yes.
In Otwock.
I have some-- an aunt in Otwock, and I was living there a while.
Otwock is not far from Warsaw.
It is 30 or 35 kilometers.
Oh, this is-- this is-- of course they had me because my family was six children.
And my-- my aunt was my-- my mother's sister.
There was only two.
So they-- [LAUGHS] they take me to help, to help the family.
It's a government school, yes.
It's what they call [NON-ENGLISH].
It means there's girls and boys in one school together.
No.
I-- I learn a little French, but-- I don't remember much.
Oh, yes.
I was always interested in in-- [? push ?] [? walking, ?] and-- and bike riding, and horse riding, and-- and being in the fields, in the woods, and this was my life.
[LAUGHS]
Oh, yes, very much, oh yes.
You can see--
Pardon me?
Oh, yes.
Yes, I love very much, horses and dogs.
I always had a dog, yes.
Oh, it was-- was-- yes, it was not very common, yes.
No.
Yes, yes.
Oh, yes.
It's not far.
Yes, I lived there.
I attend the agriculture school.
Oh, yes.
This was-- this was in Warsaw.
And it was-- it was more antisemitic than other schools.
Because the students were antisemetic.
But--
Just they don't want to sit together.
They don't want to sit together on the bench or something like this.
But-- but many didn't know I'm Jewish.
No, just boys.
I suppose so, yes.
Yes.
Oh, it's a very big-- big program studying.
We studying about-- about all-- everything what is from the land from the-- from everything.
Starting from-- from-- I couldn't tell you in-- in English.
Farming, yes.
But farming in-- in all directions, and not-- not specialized in one direction, but everything.
With crops, with cattle, with-- with-- with-- I-- I couldn't tell you.
With all branches would come from-- from the land.
Because farming in this time means a mixed farm with everything.
A farm has cows, and has horses, and has woods, and has corn, and has vegetable, everything.
Yes.
Four years.
I graduated in-- what was it?
[INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I suppose it was '32 or something.
Yes, I find a job.
It-- it's easy for me to find a job, because with many Jewish land owners, and they're always looking for-- for a Jewish agronomist.
And there was not many agronomists Jewish, so I-- I have very easy to find a job, and I was working in a few places, yes.
I was working for-- for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a man, Kohn, Oskar Kohn.
It's a big factory, but say, half a big farm, very close to the factory, a very big farm in-- in-- in Lodz.
And I was working there and even there was a kibbutz was there prepare for aliyah to-- to Israel.
And I teach them to-- to-- to-- to work in the garden, and to work in the fields, yes.
Haksharot.
Yes, it was a hakhsharot.
Yes.
My brother was electrician, Andrew.
And-- and Moshe was a bookkeeper.
Yes, they worked.
The sisters, they-- they attend school, and-- and was at home.
They're not married.
No, not married.
Yes.
Yes.
Well, I work for-- for Kohn in the-- in the-- in the farm.
And it was a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a children-- a children home.
They have a big garden.
And they-- the [? mind-- ?] the-- the director of the home was a man known now in the-- in the Jewish community, Rumkowski.
Rumkowski was very, very famous at this time.
I think he-- he later on was in the-- in the occupation, he was from the-- [NON-ENGLISH].
The Judenrat, he was head of the Judenrat.
I don't know.
It was not at this time in Lodz.
But yes, I hear about him, yes, a lot.
He was a very-- a very-- [POLISH].
I don't know the word.
[POLISH]
False.
He tells something, and he means something else.

And I remember the sisters who are working for him and they-- and they say always when people are coming, because the visitors would-- would give money for the children-- he always take two children and keeps them.
[LAUGHS] And when it's time to-- the children doesn't know when it's time to-- to leave.
And he start to-- to--
Pinch them to go away.
[LAUGHS] And he would take them, and he-- he [INAUDIBLE], yes.
He was very nasty.
He was not a nice man.
But he promise me so much when I come over, to-- I build glass houses there, and I-- and I make a-- some vegetables for the all in the fields.
And the children, I teach them how to work in the gardens.
And I teach them.
It was very-- he-- he promise me everything that I come over.
And I didn't-- I didn't work long there, and I go another place, another place was-- was a Yiddish institution.
It was [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE].
And they have a sanitorium for-- for people-- for-- for all the people, not children, for all the people there was by [?
IT ?] re-convalescent.
Like a convalescent home.
And-- and it was a big farm, with cows and horses, and-- and-- and corn field.
This was a big farm, and I was the head of the-- of the farm.
This was-- this was near Lodz, yes.
Yes.
The [INAUDIBLE] place was near Lodz, yes.
An orphanage, yes.
I know-- I would say, tell what I hear.
And later on, he take-- he took them to-- to the chamber somewhere, I don't know.
Rumkowski, I saw-- I think a picture when-- in a newspaper or somewhere [?
I remember ?] or in a film, or like a documentary film.
And when he-- he walked with the children to the chambers.
The sanatorium, it was a-- a nice building, and Jewish-- all Jewish workers there.
And I remember, there was a good friend of mine, he was the electrician.
Because they have, they have a-- a generator to run for the electricity on the whole farm.
His name was Kanisczer.
Yes.
I don't know.
I don't know.
Yes, all Jewish.
All Jewish.
It was for Jewish and-- and from Jewish people, the rich people in Lodz, where they-- where they--
Where they give money for this, only Jewish, yes.
Oh, I suppose anybody, yes.
Because Lodz was very much, very rich Jewish factories, very rich people.
Kohn was textile industry, the biggest I think who was in Poland in this time.
We call this [NON-ENGLISH].
Yes.
No.
Somebody told me the son, Oskar, went somewhere into Sweden or somewhere.
Because he was a Swedish consulate before in-- in Poland.
Yes.
But what's happened further, I don't know.
We read about it, and know about it.
And many Jews from Germany run to Poland.
And when I was in Germany later on, they told me there-- there was not a good invitation from the Polish Jews for the German Jews, who have some [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know how to tell you.
[POLISH] They are not-- not satisfied with the handling, the-- the German Jews when they come from Germany to Poland.
They were not very good, not very welcome.
Yes.
This is what they told me when I was in Germany later on.
Yes.
No.
No, I didn't.
Nothing special, what was happening I remember, it was the-- the Hitler-- I don't remember his name.
They were there in Poland-- they were-- they were hunting in Poland.
This I remember.
But for a time, until the-- the '39, when the war started.
I suppose so, yes.
Yes.
I mean up here one, a local, who run from Poland before the-- the occupation.
But he-- he passed away last year, Diamond Hirsch.
He told me he-- he went to France before.
And he told me, I was [INAUDIBLE].
He run away earlier though.
No.
Moshe was from 1933 in Israel, so he was not up here.
He was in the English army.
And in the English army, takes some-- take the Jewish boys to the army.
Because it was under occupation, English occupation.
And he was in the English army.
He was in Rome.
He was in Egypt in the English army.
And later he was in Tel Aviv and married in Tel Aviv, and stayed in Tel Aviv for all that time.
Andrew stayed in Poland, because he was in the army, in the same time that I was in the army.
He was in the front.
And when the Polish army was destroyed and they give up the-- the-- the-- their arms, they give up everything.
So he went to-- to Lvov.
He went to Lvov.
And when the-- when the German take the-- when start the-- the-- the German-Russian war start in '40-- during '42 I suppose, '42, he run further to further deep in Russia.
And he was there alone in Cheliabinsk where we are meeting later on.
Yeah, he survived.
No, not much.
Because it was very dear, and if you call it.
No, I didn't.
Not especially, no.
Before they war, I was working for a Jewish-- sanatorium.
We call this a sanatorium.
This was for children, a sanatorium.
This was run by the Bundist organization.
This was [INAUDIBLE], not far from Warsaw.
And this was the nicest time of my life.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah.
No, I was not a member, no.
But I was treated very good there.
Yes, I meet my wife there, and I married.
She worked there, yes.
She was a dietitian, yes.
Yes.
I meet-- I meet the Bundist is fair and the Bundist society they must be famous, Dr.
Ivinska.
Because she comes-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she comes from Belgium.
And I think the headquarters, of Bundist headquarters must be in Belgium, in Brussels.
And she come very often.
And she visits the sanatorium.
Yes, she took some pictures for me too, when I was in Brussels, I just forgot about it.
I visited her after the war, I visit her.
Yes.
Thank you.
[DRINKS] Yeah.
Yes.
There was Gilinski.
He was the head-- the head-- the head director, I don't know, and Brumberg, and Lazar Gilinskibotke.
It's not a hospital.
It's a sanatorium.
I think from Bundist organization.
Yeah.
No.
I was a leftist.
I was a socialist.
No I didn't, think, no.
That's right.
No.
I make a mistake.
[LAUGHS] I should at this time leave Poland.
Yes, they ask me to come with them.
Yes.
I don't know myself, some-- I don't know.
I was not ready to go.
I don't know myself.
I couldn't explain this.
Yes, yes.
Anya.
I married in-- in-- oh what was the dates-- in '30-- '39.
Yes, '38 maybe '38.
Start before-- it's just before the war started, [INAUDIBLE].
We live in Warsaw.
We live in Warsaw, close to the-- to the-- to the hospital of [INAUDIBLE].
It's a hospital we called [INAUDIBLE] Hospital.
On a street [INAUDIBLE].
And we start to-- to build up a home just for a very short time, and then everything went wrong.
When the war started, when I have to go, yes.
The first, when I-- when I-- they collect the old soldiers, the German, the German collect the soldiers, what they need.
But I was in Prague from [INAUDIBLE] the-- the-- the river in Prague.
And I have to go to Warsaw.
I have to go through the bridge, the [INAUDIBLE] Bridge.
And they're standing on the bridge, and every soldier they collect, and they put in a-- in some-- in some [INAUDIBLE], I don't how to call this.
In some place when it's first a fence around.
And they put them in, and they keep them there.
And when I was in the-- in the-- in the place where they put me, and there was a lot of soldiers.
And we started to talk what they are going to do to us.
And see, mostly say they take us to Germany to-- to-- to dig potatoes or something.
And-- and this was a time when I-- when I decide to run away.
So I jumped the fence, and-- and run away, and take off the-- the-- [NON-ENGLISH]--
The uniform, yes.
And-- and went home, and went home.
And the home was-- the whole house was bombarded.
It was [NON-ENGLISH].
There was nobody there.
